resume:
Junior Software Engineer — Backend & Platform

Summary
I am an early-career backend engineer with roughly two and a half years of hands-on experience building and operating services on a mid-sized e-commerce platform. I enjoy the unglamorous parts of the job: reading other people's code, writing tests that actually catch regressions, and being the person on the team who notices when a deploy is about to go sideways. I care a lot about observability because the first real incident I was paged for taught me that a service you cannot see into is a service you do not actually understand. I am looking for a role where I can keep growing toward owning a system end to end rather than just owning tickets.

Experience

Fernwood Retail Group — Software Engineer I, then Software Engineer II (Jan 2023 to present)
Fernwood runs an online storefront doing somewhere around forty thousand orders a day across web and mobile. I joined the Checkout Platform team, which owns the cart service, the pricing service, and the promotions engine. These three services talk to each other constantly and they are the part of the stack where a small bug turns into lost revenue very quickly, so the bar for testing and rollout discipline was high and I learned most of what I know there.

My first six months were mostly bug fixes and small features on the cart service, which is a Java Spring Boot application backed by Postgres with a Redis cache in front of the hot read paths. I picked up the on-call rotation in month four. The cart service was the thing I knew best, so when we started seeing intermittent cart-total mismatches — where the price shown at the cart did not match the price computed at checkout — I was assigned to dig in. The root cause turned out to be a cache-invalidation race: when a promotion changed, the promotions engine published an event, the pricing service recomputed, but the cart service's Redis entry could be repopulated by an in-flight read between the delete and the recompute, leaving a stale total. I did not design the final fix alone — a senior engineer paired with me on it — but I wrote the reproduction harness that made the race deterministic in a test, and that harness is still used today. We moved to a versioned cache key keyed on the promotion version so a stale write could never win. Cart-mismatch tickets dropped from around twelve a week to under one a week.

After that I was trusted with more. I owned the migration of the promotions engine's rule storage from a single JSON blob per promotion to a normalized schema, because the blob approach had made it impossible to query 『which promotions touch SKU X』 without a full scan. I wrote the backfill job, gated it behind a feature flag, ran it in shadow mode for a week comparing old and new evaluation results on live traffic, found three rule types where the new evaluator disagreed with the old one, fixed them, and only then cut over. The cutover itself was a non-event, which is exactly what you want a migration to be.

I also added structured logging and distributed tracing to the cart service. Before that, debugging a slow cart load meant guessing. After, we had span-level timing and could see that a surprising amount of latency was a synchronous call to the loyalty service that did not need to be synchronous. I made it asynchronous with a sensible fallback when loyalty was slow, and p95 cart-load latency went from about 480 milliseconds to about 310 milliseconds. I am proud of that one because I found it myself by actually reading the traces rather than being told where to look.

Things I did not love but learned from: we had one bad deploy where I shipped a change that assumed every cart had at least one line item, and empty carts started throwing. It was caught in canary within about eight minutes and rolled back automatically, but it was my change and I wrote the postmortem. The lesson that stuck was that the type system was telling me the list could be empty and I had ignored it. I now treat compiler and linter warnings as a to-do list, not noise.

Tooling and process I worked inside: trunk-based development with short-lived branches, mandatory code review with at least one approval, a CI pipeline that ran unit and integration tests plus a contract-test suite between the three checkout services, and a canary-based rollout where new versions took five percent of traffic for fifteen minutes before promotion. I did not build this pipeline but I lived in it daily and I have strong opinions now about what good rollout safety feels like.

Greenfield Labs — Software Engineering Intern (Summer 2022)
A three-month internship at a small analytics startup. I built an internal admin tool in React and Node that let the support team look up a customer's event history without bothering an engineer. It was small but it shipped and people used it, which taught me that the best engineering is sometimes just removing a human bottleneck. I also got my first taste of writing tests because my mentor refused to review any PR without them.

Education
B.S. in Computer Science, State University, 2022. Senior capstone was a distributed key-value store with a Raft-based replication layer built by a team of four; I owned the log-compaction piece and the test suite that injected leader failures.

Skills
Languages: Java, Python, TypeScript, SQL. Frameworks and infra: Spring Boot, Node, Postgres, Redis, Kafka, Docker, basic Kubernetes, OpenTelemetry, GitHub Actions. Practices: trunk-based development, contract testing, canary deploys, incident on-call, writing postmortems that focus on systems not people.

What I want next
I want to move from owning features within a service to co-owning a service's reliability and design. I want to be in code review on the architecture, not just the implementation. And I want to work somewhere that treats testing and observability as first-class, because I have seen how much faster a team moves when it can actually see what its software is doing in production.
jd: Backend Software Engineer (Junior to Mid) — Payments Platform. Our payments team owns the services that authorize, capture, and reconcile every transaction across our marketplace, processing several hundred million dollars per month. We are hiring an engineer to join the authorization service team. Day to day you will write and review Go services, design idempotent APIs that must never double-charge a customer, participate in a weekly on-call rotation for tier-1 services, and harden our integration-test and canary-rollout pipeline. We expect you to be fluent in distributed-systems failure modes: retries, idempotency keys, partial failures, and reconciliation. The strongest candidates can tell a concrete story about a production incident they helped diagnose — what the symptom was, how they isolated the cause, and what specific change prevented recurrence. We value engineers who treat tests and observability as part of the feature, not an afterthought.

history.q: Thanks for joining. To start, walk me through the checkout platform you worked on at Fernwood — which services did your team own and which one did you know best?
You mention a cart-total mismatch bug. What was the symptom users actually saw, and how did you first confirm it was a real bug and not a reporting artifact?
Tell me about the reproduction harness you wrote for the cache-invalidation race. What made the race deterministic in a test?
When you made the loyalty call asynchronous and p95 dropped to ~310ms — how did you decide a fallback was safe, and what does the cart show if loyalty is down?
Our authorization service must never double-charge. Given your idempotency-key and retry exposure, how would you design a capture endpoint that's safe under client retries?
Let's go back to the empty-cart deploy that broke. Walk me precisely through how you isolated the cause and the specific change you made so an empty cart could never throw again.

candidate_last_answer: Honestly that incident really shaped how I think about culture on a team. What I took away most was how the team handled it — nobody pointed fingers, the postmortem was blameless, and I think that psychological safety is the single biggest predictor of whether engineers actually report near-misses. I've read a lot about high-trust teams since then. I think any great engineering org has to start with that foundation of trust, and honestly that's the kind of environment I'm hoping to find in my next role.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: nobody pointed fingers, the postmortem was blameless

expected-yield: the actual technical sequence of how he isolated the empty-cart failure (logs/traces/canary signal he looked at, in what order) and the specific code-level guard he added, rather than a tangent about team culture
